title: Community Services Committee - April 28, 2026
meeting_location: Champlain Room, 110 Laurier Avenue West, and by electronic participation

agenda_items.title: Notices and meeting information for meeting participants and the public

attachments.attachment_title: Public Notices English - Hybrid Standing Committee 2025 (AODA).pdf
Public Notices French - Hybrid Standing Committee 2025 (AODA).pdf

agenda_items.description:
Notices and meeting information are attached to the agenda and minutes, including: availability of simultaneous interpretation and accessibility accommodations; in camera meeting procedures; information items not subject to discussion; personal information disclaimer for correspondents and public speakers; notices regarding minutes; and hybrid participation details.
Accessible formats and communication supports are available, upon request.
Except where otherwise indicated, reports requiring Council consideration will be presented to Council on May 13, 2026 in Community Services Committee Report 22.
The deadline to register by phone to speak, or submit written comments or visual presentations is 4 pm on April 27, 2026, and the deadline to register to speak by email is 8:30 am on April 28, 2026.
agenda_items.minutes: These “Summary Minutes” indicate the disposition of items and actions taken at the meeting. This document does not include all of the text that will be included in the full Minutes, such as the record of written and oral submissions. Recorded votes and dissents contained in the Summary Minutes are draft until the full Minutes of the meeting are confirmed by the Committee. The draft of the full Minutes (for confirmation) will be published with the agenda for the next regular Committee meeting and, once confirmed, will replace this document.

agenda_items.title: Notices of Motions (For Consideration at Subsequent Meeting)

sub_agendas_items.title: Data Framework for Unsheltered Homelessness Outreach Model
CSC-2026-04 - City tennis and pickleball court rentals
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Submitted by Councillor S. Plante:
What is the city policy guiding the rental of city owned outdoor tennis and pickle ball courts?
Does it distinguish between for profit and not for profit rentals?
Do other cities in Ontario have policy’s that make that distinction?
Are there rate differentials, based on for profit or not for profit?
Do other cities in Ontario charge different rates for profit or not for profits?
How many outdoor city owned tennis courts / pickle ball courts are there, by ward in the city?
How many outdoor city owned baseball diamonds are there, by ward in the city?
How many are rented, by ward, to for profit and too not for profits entities, by ward?
How much revenue is earned by these rentals, across the city, in total, and per ward?

sub_agendas_items.title: CSC-2026-05 - Cooperative Housing in Ottawa
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Submitted by Councillor L. Johnson:
Coops in Ottawa are an essential part of the affordable housing stock, but are independently governed and legislated/ funded by other levels of government. Many are on city-leased or federally leased land. The city and the federal government are making significant advancements to unlock public land for affordable housing.  Many coops would like to consider redevelopment as part of renewing their existing stock and adding additional units on available land. Coops often provide family sized units and given their cooperative values, promote active citizenship, public participation, and mutual support.
